content.text:
{Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever.}{Hebrews 13:8}
 {Eigenvalues are the fundamental constants of a linear transformation.}{— David Lay}

An **eigenvector** is a nonzero vector that is only scaled, not 
rotated, by a linear transformation.

content.title: Eigenvalue and Eigenvector
content.content:
Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. A scalar $\lambda$ is an 
**eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a 
nonzero vector $\vect{v}$ such that
\[
A\vect{v} = \lambda\vect{v}
\]
Any such nonzero vector $\vect{v}$ is called an 
**eigenvector** corresponding to $\lambda$.

content.text:
The equation says: applying $A$ to $\vect{v}$ gives the same result 
as multiplying $\vect{v}$ by the scalar $\lambda$. The eigenvector 
points in a "privileged" direction that $A$ preserves.

content.title: Verifying eigenvectors
content.problem:
Show that $\vect{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is an 
eigenvector of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$.

content.title: Eigenspace
content.content:
The **eigenspace** of $A$ corresponding to 
eigenvalue $\lambda$ is:
\[
E_\lambda = \Null(A - \lambda I) = \{\vect{v} : A\vect{v} = \lambda\vect{v}\}
\]
It is the set of all eigenvectors for $\lambda$, plus the zero vector.

marginNotes.text:
But you are the same, and your years 
have no end.
